prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
Here’s one: You probably overestimate how much other people notice or care about your mistakes, appearance, or social missteps.

Most of us walk around convinced that our stumbles (a awkward comment, a bad hair day, a forgotten name) are glaringly obvious to everyone—when in reality, people are too wrapped up in their own lives to register them. Psychologists call this the **"spotlight effect,"** and it’s a near-universal bias.

The stranger writing to me right now? Odds are you’ve replayed some minor embarrassment from years ago that no one else remembers. (I’ve done it too.) The freedom in realizing this is that you can stop performing so hard. Most of the time, you’re your own harshest audience.
